name: Academic Advising Center
shortDescription: Personalized academic planning and degree guidance for all students.
fullDescription: The Academic Advising Center provides one-on-one advising sessions to help students plan course schedules, navigate degree requirements, explore majors and minors, and stay on track for graduation. Walk-in hours and appointments are available year-round.
aiContextNotes: Serves all undergraduate and graduate students. Especially useful for freshmen, transfer students, and those exploring major changes.

name: Counseling and Wellness Center
shortDescription: Free mental health counseling, crisis support, and wellness programs.
fullDescription: The Counseling and Wellness Center offers individual therapy, group therapy, crisis intervention, and wellness workshops at no cost to enrolled students. Services include anxiety and stress management, grief counseling, and substance use support. Telehealth options are available.
aiContextNotes: Confidential services. Crisis hotline available 24/7. Popular wellness workshops include mindfulness and time management.

name: Career Development Office
shortDescription: Career coaching, resume reviews, internship placement, and job fairs.
fullDescription: The Career Development Office supports students in building professional skills through resume and cover letter workshops, mock interviews, career fairs, and internship/job placement assistance. Employers from tech, finance, healthcare, and more recruit on campus each semester.
aiContextNotes: Hosts two major career fairs per year (fall and spring). Strong employer connections in tech and engineering sectors.

name: Disability and Accessibility Services
shortDescription: Accommodations and support for students with disabilities.
fullDescription: Disability and Accessibility Services coordinates academic accommodations such as extended test time, note-taking services, assistive technology, and accessible housing. The office works with faculty to ensure equal access to educational programs and activities.
aiContextNotes: Students need to provide documentation to register. Early registration each semester is recommended for accommodation setup.

name: Writing Center
shortDescription: Free writing tutoring for essays, research papers, and applications.
fullDescription: The Writing Center offers free peer tutoring for any writing project—from course essays and research papers to graduate theses and scholarship applications. Both in-person and online sessions are available by appointment or walk-in.
aiContextNotes: Busiest during midterms and finals. Online sessions available evenings and weekends.

name: Machine Intelligence Lab
shortDescription: Research lab focused on deep learning, NLP, and computer vision.
fullDescription: The Machine Intelligence Lab conducts cutting-edge research in deep learning architectures, natural language processing, and computer vision. Current projects include large language model fine-tuning, medical image analysis, and multimodal learning. The lab publishes at top venues including NeurIPS, ICML, and CVPR.
aiContextNotes: Led by Dr. Sarah Chen. Accepts undergrad RAs with strong ML coursework. Weekly reading group open to all students.

name: Autonomous Robotics Laboratory
shortDescription: Building intelligent robots for navigation, manipulation, and human-robot interaction.
fullDescription: The Autonomous Robotics Laboratory develops robots capable of autonomous navigation, dexterous manipulation, and natural human-robot interaction. Research areas include reinforcement learning for robot control, SLAM, and collaborative robotics in manufacturing and healthcare settings.
aiContextNotes: Joint lab between ME and CS departments. Has several robot platforms including mobile robots and a 7-DOF arm. Undergrad positions available each semester.

name: Human-Computer Interaction Lab
shortDescription: Research on user interfaces, accessibility tech, and interactive systems.
fullDescription: The HCI Lab explores how people interact with computing systems, with active projects in accessible technology design, AR/VR interfaces, voice user interfaces, and design methods for inclusive software. The lab collaborates with the School of Design and local industry partners.
audience: Students interested in UX, design, and accessibility research
aiContextNotes: Interdisciplinary lab welcoming students from CS, design, and psychology. Runs user studies regularly and needs participants and research assistants.

name: Biomedical Computing Lab
shortDescription: Applying computational methods to biomedical data and health informatics.
fullDescription: The Biomedical Computing Lab applies machine learning, data mining, and statistical modeling to health data including genomics, medical imaging, and electronic health records. Current projects include cancer risk prediction, drug interaction modeling, and wearable health monitoring.
audience: Graduate students; undergrads with biology + CS background
aiContextNotes: Interdisciplinary — welcomes students from CS, biology, and BME. Strong publication record in bioinformatics journals.

name: Cybersecurity Research Lab
shortDescription: Research on network security, cryptography, and privacy-preserving systems.
fullDescription: The Cybersecurity Research Lab focuses on network intrusion detection, applied cryptography, secure software development, and privacy-preserving computation. The lab partners with government agencies and industry on real-world security challenges and hosts an annual campus CTF competition.
aiContextNotes: Hosts annual CTF competition open to all students. Good entry point for undergrads interested in security. Industry-funded research positions available.

name: Dr. Sarah Chen — Machine Learning
shortDescription: Leading researcher in deep learning, NLP, and multimodal AI systems.
fullDescription: Dr. Sarah Chen is an Associate Professor in Computer Science specializing in deep learning and natural language processing. She leads the Machine Intelligence Lab and has published 50+ papers at top AI conferences. Her current focus areas include LLM alignment, multimodal learning, and efficient inference. She advises both MS and PhD students and occasionally mentors undergraduates.
audience: Graduate students; advanced undergrads with ML coursework
aiContextNotes: Very responsive to well-written cold emails. Prefers students who have taken at least one ML course. Office hours Tuesdays 2-4pm.

name: Dr. James Park — Robotics
shortDescription: Expert in autonomous systems, robot learning, and human-robot interaction.
fullDescription: Dr. James Park is a Professor of Mechanical Engineering and Computer Science who directs the Autonomous Robotics Laboratory. His research spans robot learning, motion planning, and collaborative robotics. He is known for his hands-on lab culture and enthusiasm for mentoring undergraduate researchers.
aiContextNotes: Very welcoming to undergrads. Runs a robotics bootcamp each January. Good first research experience for students new to robotics.

name: Dr. Aisha Patel — HCI & Accessibility
shortDescription: Researcher in human-computer interaction, accessible design, and AR/VR.
fullDescription: Dr. Aisha Patel is an Assistant Professor working at the intersection of computer science and design. Her research focuses on making technology more accessible and inclusive, with active projects in AR/VR interfaces, voice interaction, and participatory design methods. She co-directs the HCI Lab.
audience: Students from CS, design, psychology, and related fields
aiContextNotes: Welcomes students from non-CS backgrounds. Active in mentoring first-generation students. Participatory design projects often need research assistants.

name: Dr. Michael Torres — Biomedical AI
shortDescription: Applying AI and data science to healthcare and genomics.
fullDescription: Dr. Michael Torres is a Professor of Biomedical Engineering whose lab develops computational tools for precision medicine. His work includes cancer risk modeling, drug-gene interaction prediction, and real-time health monitoring using wearable devices. He collaborates with the university hospital.
audience: Students with background in CS, biology, or BME
aiContextNotes: Interdisciplinary focus. Students should have basic programming skills. Summer research positions available through the BME department.

name: Dr. Elena Vasquez — Cybersecurity
shortDescription: Expert in network security, applied cryptography, and privacy systems.
fullDescription: Dr. Elena Vasquez is an Associate Professor in Computer Science leading the Cybersecurity Research Lab. She has extensive industry experience and government collaborations. Her research areas include intrusion detection systems, post-quantum cryptography, and differential privacy. She organizes the annual campus CTF.
aiContextNotes: Industry connections are strong — many of her students intern at security firms. CTF competition is a great entry point.

name: Undergraduate Research Fellowship Program
shortDescription: Paid semester-long research positions for undergraduates across all STEM departments.
fullDescription: The Undergraduate Research Fellowship provides stipends of $3,000 per semester for students to work 10 hours/week in a faculty research lab. Fellows present their work at the annual undergraduate research symposium. Applications open each October for spring and March for fall.
audience: Undergraduates in any STEM major with 3.0+ GPA
aiContextNotes: Competitive but accessible. Faculty sponsors required — students should identify a lab and professor first. Strong personal statements matter.

name: LaunchPad Startup Incubator
shortDescription: Campus incubator offering mentorship, workspace, and seed funding for student startups.
fullDescription: LaunchPad is the university's student startup incubator, providing co-working space, mentorship from entrepreneurs and VCs, workshops on business development, and up to $10,000 in seed funding through a competitive pitch process. Teams at any stage are welcome to apply.
aiContextNotes: Open to all majors, not just business students. Many successful tech startups have come from this program. Demo days held each semester.

name: Annual Innovation Hackathon
shortDescription: 48-hour hackathon with prizes, mentors, and industry sponsors.
fullDescription: The Annual Innovation Hackathon brings together 500+ students for a weekend of building. Participants form teams, choose challenges from sponsor companies, and build working prototypes. Prizes include cash awards, internship interviews, and cloud computing credits. Beginners and experienced builders are all welcome.
aiContextNotes: Usually held in late October. Registration opens mid-September. Great for first-time hackers — workshops run in parallel.

name: Teaching Assistant Positions
shortDescription: Paid TA roles in CS, engineering, and science departments.
fullDescription: Teaching assistant positions are available each semester across STEM departments. TAs lead lab sections, hold office hours, grade assignments, and support faculty. Compensation includes a stipend and/or tuition credit. Strong academic performance in the relevant course is required.
aiContextNotes: Applications typically due 4-6 weeks before semester start. Prior experience as a tutor or course assistant is helpful.

name: ACM Student Chapter
shortDescription: Student-run computing club with workshops, talks, and competitive programming.
fullDescription: The ACM Student Chapter is the largest computing club on campus, hosting weekly tech talks, coding workshops, competitive programming practice, and social events. Special interest groups (SIGs) cover web development, AI/ML, game development, and cybersecurity. No experience required to join.
aiContextNotes: Meetings every Thursday 6pm. Great way to meet other CS students and learn new skills. Hosts study sessions before exams.

name: Women in Computer Science (WiCS)
shortDescription: Community and professional development for women and non-binary students in tech.
fullDescription: Women in Computer Science (WiCS) provides mentorship, networking, and professional development for women and non-binary students pursuing computing careers. Events include industry panels, resume workshops, coding nights, and an annual conference trip. Allies are welcome at most events.
audience: Women, non-binary students, and allies in computing
aiContextNotes: Strong industry sponsor connections — members get early access to internship postings. Mentorship pairings happen at semester start.

name: Robotics Club
shortDescription: Hands-on robotics building, competitions, and community.
fullDescription: The Robotics Club is a project-based student organization where members design, build, and program robots for competitions and fun. Teams compete in regional and national events including RoboSub, FIRST Robotics alumni events, and custom challenge courses. Members learn CAD, embedded programming, and mechanical design.
audience: All students — no prior experience needed
aiContextNotes: Great hands-on complement to the academic Robotics Lab. Members can access the maker space for personal projects too.

name: Data Science Society
shortDescription: Student community for data science, analytics, and applied ML projects.
fullDescription: The Data Science Society organizes workshops on Python, R, SQL, and ML tools; hosts Kaggle competition teams; and runs semester-long applied data projects with real datasets from campus partners. Members build portfolios and connect with data professionals through speaker events.
aiContextNotes: Beginner-friendly workshops at start of each semester. Applied projects are a great way to build a data science portfolio.

name: Enactus Social Entrepreneurship Team
shortDescription: Student team building social impact projects and competing nationally.
fullDescription: Enactus is a global student organization focused on using entrepreneurial action to improve communities. The campus chapter runs projects addressing food insecurity, financial literacy, and sustainability. Teams compete at regional and national Enactus competitions judged by business leaders.
audience: All students interested in social impact
aiContextNotes: Cross-disciplinary — draws students from business, engineering, social sciences. Good for students wanting impact-focused experience.

name: International Student Services
shortDescription: Immigration advising, cultural programs, and support for international students.
fullDescription: International Student Services assists with visa and immigration advising, cultural adjustment support, and social programming. The office hosts orientation for new international students, runs a conversation partners program, and provides tax filing workshops for non-resident students.
aiContextNotes: Critical resource for visa-related questions. OPT/CPT workshops held each semester. Peer mentor program for new arrivals.

name: Summer Research Experience for Undergraduates (REU)
shortDescription: NSF-funded 10-week summer research program with stipend and housing.
fullDescription: The Summer REU program, funded by the National Science Foundation, provides a $6,000 stipend, campus housing, and research mentorship for 10 weeks over the summer. Students work closely with a faculty mentor on an active research project and present findings at a symposium. Open to students from any accredited university.
audience: Undergraduates from any university (US citizens/permanent residents)
aiContextNotes: Highly competitive. Applications due in February. Strong recommendation letters and a research interest statement are key.
